stat_text: Response: Total expenditures for public elementary and secondary schools in the United States were $927 billion in 202021 (in constant 202223 dollars).
source_title: NCES — Per Pupil Expenditure in Public Schools

stat_text: 1 , 2 , 3 This amounts to an average of $18,614 per public school pupil enrolled in the fall of that school year.
source_title: NCES — Per Pupil Expenditure in Public Schools

stat_text: In 2020–21, of the $18,614 spent on total expenditures per pupil nationally, current expenditures—which include salaries, employee benefits, purchased services, supplies, tuition, and other expenditures—accounted for $16,280 (87 percent); capital outlay—which includes expenditures for property, buildings, and alterations completed by school district staff or contractors—accounted for $1,833 (10 percent); and interest on school debt accounted for $501 (3 percent).
source_title: NCES — Per Pupil Expenditure in Public Schools

stat_text: Average current expenditures per pupil enrolled in the fall in public elementary and secondary schools increased by 13 percent from 2010–11 ($14,453) to 2020–21 ($16,280), after adjusting for inflation.
source_title: NCES — Per Pupil Expenditure in Public Schools

stat_text: During this period, current expenditures per pupil were lowest in 2012–13 ($13,952) and then increased each year from 2012–13 to 2020–21.
source_title: NCES — Per Pupil Expenditure in Public Schools

stat_text: From 2010–11 to 2020–21, the percentage of current expenditures spent on staff salaries decreased from 59 to 55 percent.
source_title: NCES — Per Pupil Expenditure in Public Schools

stat_text: In contrast, the percentage of current expenditures spent on employee benefits increased from 21 to 24 percent during this period.
source_title: NCES — Per Pupil Expenditure in Public Schools

stat_text: Altogether, salaries and benefits combined accounted for 79 to 80 percent of current expenditures throughout the decade.
source_title: NCES — Per Pupil Expenditure in Public Schools

stat_text: Apart from salaries and benefits, current expenditures in 2020–21 were spent as follows: 11 percent on purchased services, which include items such as contracts for food, transportation, janitorial services, and professional development for teachers; 8 percent on supplies, which range from books to heating oil; and 2 percent combined on tuition and other expenditures.
source_title: NCES — Per Pupil Expenditure in Public Schools

stat_text: The percentage of current expenditures spent on each of these objects in 2020–21 differed by 1 percentage point or less compared with the corresponding percentage in 2010–11.
source_title: NCES — Per Pupil Expenditure in Public Schools

stat_text: During 161 fishing events, tuna catches amounted to 147 t: 72% by weight was skipjack, 25% yellowfin tuna ( Thunnus albacares) and 3% other tunas.
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Bycatch (all non-tuna species caught plus all tuna discards) amounted to 951 kg (0.65% of total tuna catch).
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Most of the bycatch (95%) was utilized, and some bycatch was released alive, so dead discards were particularly low (0.02% of total tuna catch, or 22 kg per 100 t).
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Rainbow runner ( Elagatis bipinnulata ) and dolphinfish ( Coryphaena hippurus ) together constituted 93% of the bycatch.
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: A recent global review suggested that discards averaged 10.3 Mt per year during 2000–2010 [ 2 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Catches of the three main tropical species amounted to 527,447 t in 2015, of which yellowfin tuna ( Thunnus albacares ) accounted for 45%, followed closely by skipjack tuna ( Katsuwonus pelamis ) (44%), and catches of bigeye tuna ( Thunnus obesus ) account for the remaining 11%.
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: The major tuna fishing gears within the entire Indian Ocean (both tropical and temperate) are purse seine (26% of tuna catch during 2015), gillnet (33%), longline (16%) and pole-and-line (6%), with the balance taken by minor gears or unrecorded gear types (tuna and effort catch data from www.iotc.org , accessed January 2017).
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: The Maldives boasts the highest per capita fish consumption in the world [ 21 ], with over half of all fish caught consumed locally rather than exported (53%, or 64,000 t in 2012) [ 22 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Species other than tunas (primarily rainbow runner, silky shark [ Carcharhinus falciformis ] and dolphinfish [ Coryphaena hippurus ]) probably contributed ‘less than 5% of the total catch’ from the pole-and-line fishery [ 26 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Approximately 50% of the Maldivian pole-and-line tuna catch is currently taken from these aFADs [ 39 ], up from an estimated 44% in the early 1990s [ 40 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Fishing events were defined as periods of active fishing that were separated by more than 10 minutes.
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: The main tuna species taken were skipjack (72.3% of the tuna catch by weight) and yellowfin (25.0%), with bigeye, kawakawa, and frigate tuna together making up the balance (2.7%) ( Table 2 ).
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: That was 0.65% of the tuna landings, or 0.64% of all catch and bycatch.
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Bycatch was taken in just 5.4% of all free school fishing events, but in 52% of all associated school fishing events.
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Furthermore, bycatch from free schools was only 0.02% of the tuna catch by weight, whereas from associated schools it was 1.21%, and the bycatch rate was significantly higher at all associated schools (U = 995.5, p <0.0001).
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Note that 55% of the fishing events (and 33% of catch) occurred at aFADs ( Table 1 ).
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Live releases amounted to 0.01% of the tuna catch or 2% of bycatch, while dead discards were 0.02% of the tuna catch (3% of bycatch).
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Discards amounted to 32 kg, equivalent to 22 kg per 100 t of tuna catch, or 0.02% of tuna catch.
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0177391.t008 Discussion The bycatch rate recorded here (0.65% of the tuna catch) is very similar to that recorded from western Pacific pole-and-line tuna fisheries.
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: In a review of the limited and scattered literature, average bycatch was estimated at 0.8% of the total catch for western Pacific pole-and-line skipjack fisheries, with rainbow runner and dolphinfish being the most frequently taken bycatch species [ 43 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Observer data from the Solomon Islands gave an estimated bycatch of 0.6% of the pole-and-line tuna catch, with rainbow runner and dolphinfish again being the most frequently taken bycatch species [ 44 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: In Indonesia, bycatch was estimated at 1% in small-scale tuna pole-and-line fisheries [ 4 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: In a global review, a mean discard rate of 0.4% from tuna pole-and-line fisheries was estimated [ 15 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Indeed, purse seine bycatch ranged from a low of 0.9% for seamount-associated schools in the Indian Ocean [ 9 ] to 15.0% for FAD-associated schools in the Atlantic [ 55 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: One exception to this standard practice was provided by [ 25 ], wherein livebait was included in bycatch estimates for the Maldivian pole-and-line fishery and, based on published information from MRC, noted bycatch at 11.6% of the tuna catch.
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: That was equivalent to 8.9% of the tuna catch, or 11.3 kg of tuna per 1 kg of livebait caught.
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: This is comparable to, but slightly more efficient than, catch rates estimated by previous Maldives studies (summarised in [ 36 ]), which reported catches within the range of 7.4–10.0 kg of tuna per 1 kg of livebait used, or 10.0–13.5% of tuna catch.
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Skipjack made up nearly all (98%) the tuna catch from free schools but only half (51%) from associated schools.
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Also, across the entire study, skipjack contributed 72.3% to the total tuna catch, which is close to the long-term estimated average for the national fishery of 75% [ 59 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: In this study, the proportion of bigeye in the Thunnus component of the pole-and-line catch was estimated at 6.1%, but with a higher proportion in the south (7.3%) than the north (2.7%) ( S7 Table ).
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: In neighbouring Sri Lanka, inspection of ‘small yellowfin’ landings at Beruwela (6°29’N, the same latitude as Shaviyani Atoll in the north of Maldives) found 0.7% bigeye [ 64 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Comparison with other fisheries In comparison to other fisheries, a bycatch rate of 0.5%-1% is very low.
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: For example, there are estimates of global average bycatch from shrimp trawl of 62%, tuna longline of 28%, and tuna purse seine at 5% [ 15 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: In 2015, reported catches for tuna and tuna-like species were 534,000 t for gillnet (33% of the total), 415,000 t for purse seine (26%) and 260,000 t for longline (16%), compared to 102,000 t for pole-and-line (6%) (catch data from www.iotc.org , accessed January 2017).
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: However, recent research in Pakistan, which incorporated observer data, reported bycatch amounting roughly 40% of tuna landings [ 67 , 68 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: For these fleets, bycatch was sampled during 2003–2009 by onboard observers, and estimated at 11,590 t per year, which was 4.7% of tuna landings [ 69 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: Nevertheless, if 4.7% were representative of all Indian Ocean tuna purse seine fisheries, then it would suggest bycatch of over 20,000 t in 2015.
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: However, a large study of bycatch on Taiwanese tuna longliners which covered much of the Indian Ocean found that bycatch and discards comprised 35.8% of total catch [ 13 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: And for the European purse seine fishery, it was reported that tuna discards accounted for 54% of all bycatch [ 9 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: In comparison, longline tuna fishing can have high mortality rates of discarded fishes: 38–63% discarded dead for Taiwanese tuna longline fisheries [ 13 ], and 59% discarded dead for western-central Pacific longline fisheries [ 44 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: In one study for Indian Ocean purse seine fishery, over 50% of the silky sharks brought on board in purse seine operations and released alive subsequently died, raising the total mortality rate of brailed sharks to 85% [ 79 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: FADs & unobserved mortalities As noted above, the Maldivian pole-and-line fishery makes full use of the network of approximately 50 aFADs deployed around the atolls [ 81 , 82 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: In the western Indian Ocean purse seine fisheries, very high numbers of dFADs are employed: more than 7,600 dFADs were estimated to have been deployed in the Indian Ocean in 2009 [ 83 ], and 6,700 active dFADs were deployed by the French and Spanish purse seine fleets in the western Indian Ocean at the end of 2013 [ 84 ].
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: The number of Maldivian aFADs is less than 1% of the number of dFADs deployed by the western Indian Ocean purse seine fisheries.
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children

stat_text: However, the Maldivian pole-and-line fishery has few discards, since most (95%) of the non-tuna catch is edible and is utilised.
source_title: PLOS ONE — Social Development in Homeschooled vs. Traditionally Schooled Children
